narrative: As a white male applicant with an upper-middle-class family background, your law school application was evaluated using a predictive model that assesses the likelihood of first-time success on the bar exam, based on profiles of past students. The model estimated a 69.3% probability that you would fail the bar exam on your first attempt, which is high enough to trigger an automatic rejection of your admission to protect the school's outcomes and reputation. This decision hinged primarily on your early academic performance in school, where your Year 3 grades placed you in the bottom decile—far below the typical student's middle-of-the-pack standing—which strongly signaled to the model a heightened risk of bar failure. Your Year 1 grades, also in the bottom decile and well under the average, reinforced this concern as the second-biggest factor pushing the prediction toward failure. While your undergraduate GPA of 3.00 is slightly below the dataset average of 3.21, it actually worked in your favor, nudging the model's assessment slightly toward expecting bar passage. Other elements, like your LSAT score of 34 compared to the usual 36.7, or your plan to attend full-time like most applicants, played lesser roles in this outcome. In essence, the model saw your consistently low early decile rankings as the dominant red flags when stacked against students who typically pass the bar, outweighing the modest positives. This isn't a judgment on your potential or background—many from similar upper-middle-income families succeed—but a data-driven flag on those specific early hurdles. To move forward, consider retaking courses or programs to boost your decile rankings, aiming for top-half performance that aligns with bar passers. Strengthening your undergraduate record through post-baccalaureate options or explaining context in a new application could also help. Retest the LSAT to close that gap to average, and gather letters highlighting your resilience as a full-time committed student. Many applicants in your position reapply successfully after targeted improvements—your next steps can rewrite this story.
